serp_snapshot.title: 40 AI book writing prompts: Ideas to start your next story (AI at Meta)
serp_snapshot.notes: The authority competitor. 40 prompts in 8 use-case categories (plot, character, worldbuilding, theme, dialogue, etc.) as use-case + example-prompt tables. High authority but Meta-AI-branded and generic examples; no reusable template + why-it-works, no anti-generic guidance.

serp_snapshot.title: ChatGPT for Authors: 30+ Prompts to Help Write Your Next Book (Thinkific)
serp_snapshot.notes: Direct format rival. Use-case + example-prompt table (outline, chapters, plot, setting, even publisher outreach). ChatGPT-centric and book-generic (skews nonfiction-friendly); prompts are one-off examples, not parameterized templates.

serp_snapshot.title: The Fiction Writing With AI Prompt Library — 75+ prompts (Write With AI / Substack)
serp_snapshot.notes: PAID Notion library (75+ prompts) behind a Substack paywall. Strong 'AI-assisted not AI-generated' framing. A free, high-quality, copy-paste set on a blog directly undercuts this.

serp_snapshot.title: Please share your writing prompts to get the best prose from AI (Reddit r/WritingWithAI)
serp_snapshot.notes: Real practitioner demand + the framework that works: context (story bible/profiles) + role + constraints + format; feed the AI the actual manuscript; work in 1-2k-word chunks. Confirms readers want REUSABLE structure, not random example lines.

serp_snapshot.title: AI prompts for sharper fiction editing for writers (OpenAI Developer Community)
serp_snapshot.notes: Power-user revision prompt: 'write like a structural engineer inspecting a bridge', issue IDs, severity, minimal patch, world-rules ledger. Shows the high end of what a revision prompt can do; nothing in the listicles matches this depth.

serp_snapshot.title: AI and Fiction: A Writer's Tool, Not a Crutch (Timothy Patterson / Substack)
serp_snapshot.notes: Use-case essay (plot brainstorming, character exploration, worldbuilding, scene outlining) with the 'stay in control' stance. Confirms the trusted framing but gives few copy-paste prompts.

serp_snapshot.title: 10 ways AI can help writers (Royal Literary Fund)
serp_snapshot.notes: High-authority literary body. A few parameterized prompts with X-slots ('write X words of flash fiction on X'). Balanced, voice-preserving tone. General writing, not a fiction prompt pack.

serp_snapshot.title: Best Writing Tools for Fiction Authors (2026): AI and Beyond (LaterPress)
serp_snapshot.notes: Tools listicle ranking for the adjacent commercial query (Sudowrite/NovelCrafter/Squibler/SidekickWriter). Shows the prompt query sits next to the tools query — natural funnel to our Cluster B.

related_queries: ChatGPT prompts for writing a book
best AI prompts for creative writing
how to prompt AI to write a story
AI prompts to edit a novel

secondary_keywords.keyword: ChatGPT prompts for writing a book
best AI tools for fiction writers

content_gaps: Almost every ranking page is a flat list of one-off EXAMPLE prompts ('write a plot about a small town...'). Nobody gives REUSABLE, parameterized templates with [bracketed slots] + a one-line 'why this works / how to push it harder'. That's the single biggest differentiator.
The best libraries (75+ prompts) are PAYWALLED (Substack/Notion). A free, copy-paste, fiction-specific set on a blog beats them on access.

The listicles organize by loose use-case but not by the actual WRITING WORKFLOW. Organizing the prompts by stage (idea -> plot -> character -> world -> scene -> dialogue -> revision) lets this post funnel straight UP to the new Pillar 1 (the 7-stage workflow) and ACROSS to A2/A3.
Most are ChatGPT- or Meta-AI-branded; a tool-agnostic pack (works in ChatGPT/Claude/Sudowrite/NovelCrafter) serves more readers.

Nobody answers the real failure: the prompts produce GENERIC output. A short 'how to keep these from sounding like AI' section (paste a voice sample, reject the first answer, edit pass) is on-brand with our de-AI gate and absent from competitors.
recommended_angle: '50+ AI Story Prompts for Fiction Writers (Copy-Paste, by Stage)'. A free, tool-agnostic prompt PACK organized to mirror Pillar 1's workflow: idea/premise, plot & structure, character, worldbuilding, scene drafting, dialogue, revision. Each prompt is copy-paste-ready with [bracketed slots] and a one-line note on why it works and how to push it (reject-the-safe-answer). Open by setting intent (these are prompts you give an AI as a collaborator, you stay the author) and close with a short 'keep them from sounding generic' section (voice sample + edit pass). Funnel UP to {how-to-write-a-novel-with-ai}, ACROSS to {how-to-develop-fictional-characters-with-ai} (character section) and {overcome-writers-block-with-ai} (idea section), and to the tools posts at the drafting/consistency moment. Informational, ads, funnels to affiliate.
reason: PURSUE. Clean informational intent with a concrete copy-paste job-to-be-done, and a winnable SERP: the top results are generic example-prompt listicles (Meta, Thinkific), paywalled libraries (Substack), and forum threads. None offers a free, fiction-specific, tool-agnostic pack of REUSABLE parameterized templates organized by writing stage with anti-generic guidance. That gap plus the funnel fit (organize by Pillar 1's stages -> links straight up to the new hub and across to A2/A3, and to the tools cluster at drafting) makes it a strong Cluster-A addition. Difficulty medium (Meta is high authority but its piece is a generic list). Dominant intent = prompts you feed an AI to develop a story; the 'AI-generated writing prompts for humans' reading is secondary and noted.
